claim: Multiple identical instances of a product are perceived as more unified and homogeneous, increasing perceived efficacy of each unit.

phenomenon: goal conflict salience in time allocation
claim: Perceiving conflict between personal goals heightens reliance on easily justifiable reasons, biasing subsequent unrelated time allocation toward work over leisure.

phenomenon: financial constraint framing in discretionary spending
claim: Financial constraints shift focus toward purchase durability and future benefit streams, reversing the typical preference for experiences over material goods.

phenomenon: economic condition effects on parental investment
claim: Poor economic conditions increase parental spending on daughters because daughters have more assured reproductive outcomes than sons under resource scarcity.

phenomenon: logo shape influence on product attributes
claim: Circular versus angular logo shapes activate softness and hardness concepts, biasing mental imagery of products toward corresponding attributes.

phenomenon: lateral display position influence on food choice
claim: Displaying healthy items to the left of unhealthy items aligns with natural left-to-right magnitude representation, increasing processing ease and healthy choice likelihood.
